planting.note: Plant offsets or container stock in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, gritty to sandy soil in full sun to part shade; Mexican snowball requires perfect drainage and is intolerant of wet soil, especially in the center of the rosette — the most common cause of death is crown rot from standing water.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, gritty to sandy soil or succulent potting mix in bright sun to part shade; allow soil to dry completely between waterings. Echeveria elegans (Mexican snowball) is one of the classic and most widely grown echeverias — a Mexican succulent producing perfect, symmetrical, pale-blue-grey to ice-blue rosettes of dense, fleshy, spoon-shaped leaves 4–6 inches across, with a powdery glaucous coating that gives the rosette a cool, icy-blue appearance and inspired the common name 'Mexican snowball.' In spring and summer, it produces arching, coral-pink flower stems with nodding, yellow-tipped, pink flowers that are attractive to hummingbirds. The plant offsets freely (producing 'chicks' around the central 'hen'), allowing easy propagation by division; leaf and stem cuttings also root readily. Echeveria elegans is the foundation of much of the interest in succulent container gardening and succulent wreaths, and is one of the best echeverias for humid climates (it tolerates more moisture than many succulents if drainage is excellent). In zone 9, it grows outdoors in well-drained rockeries, raised beds, and container gardens; bring indoors during hard freezes.
sources: Cactus and Succulent Society of America
University of California Cooperative Extension — Succulent Plants

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile succulent potting mix in bright indirect light to part shade; florist kalanchoe is a short-day plant — it requires 6+ weeks of days shorter than 12 hours to initiate flowering, which occurs naturally in fall/winter.

directions: Grow in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral succulent potting mix in bright indirect light; allow to dry between waterings; provide 6+ weeks of short days (less than 12 hours) to induce re-flowering. Kalanchoe blossfeldiana (florist kalanchoe or Christmas kalanchoe) is one of the world's best-selling potted plants — a Madagascar succulent producing a compact mound of glossy, scallop-edged, fleshy, dark-green leaves and dense, upright clusters of long-lasting, 4-petaled flowers in red, orange, yellow, salmon, pink, white, and purple — flowers that remain fresh for 6–8 weeks or more on the plant. The plants are 'forced' into bloom by commercial growers by manipulating photoperiod (day length); at home, allowing plants to experience natural short days in fall and early winter will trigger re-blooming. In zones 10–12, kalanchoe can be grown outdoors in frost-free, well-drained locations as a tender perennial; in zone 9 and cooler, it is an outstanding houseplant and winter gift plant. After blooming, cut back flower stalks and reduce water; resume normal watering and fertilizing in spring. CAUTION: Toxic to cats and dogs.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Houseplants

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings or container stock in spring in very well-drained, lean, gritty, slightly alkaline to neutral succulent potting mix in bright indirect light to part sun; string of pearls requires perfectly draining substrate and infrequent watering — the spherical leaves store water and will rot quickly if kept wet.

directions: Plant stem cuttings in very well-drained, lean, gritty succulent mix in bright indirect light; water only when soil is completely dry. Senecio rowleyanus (string of pearls) is one of the most distinctive and sought-after trailing succulents in cultivation — a South African native producing trailing stems up to 2–3 feet long densely clothed in perfectly round, pea-sized, green, bead-like succulent leaves with a distinctive translucent stripe on each sphere. The spherical leaf shape is a remarkable adaptation for water storage and light transmission: the round form minimizes the surface-area-to-volume ratio (reducing water loss) while the translucent stripe allows light to penetrate to the interior of the leaf for photosynthesis. The resulting trailing curtains of pearl-like leaves cascading from hanging baskets or trailing over pot edges are one of the most charming and photogenic effects in indoor or outdoor succulent gardening. In late winter, plants may produce small, white, cinnamon-scented flowers. CAUTION: Toxic to cats and dogs if ingested. String of pearls requires careful watering — the most common error is overwatering; allow soil to dry completely between waterings.
sources: Cactus and Succulent Society of America
University of California Cooperative Extension — Succulents

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings or container stock in fall or spring in well-drained, moderately lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy-loam soil in full sun to part shade; aeoniums are winter-growing, summer-dormant succulents from the Canary Islands that prefer the opposite season from most succulents — they grow actively in cool, moist winters and go dormant in hot, dry summers.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; water moderately in fall through spring; withhold water in summer when the plant is dormant. Aeonium arboreum (tree aeonium or Irish rose) is a Canary Islands succulent remarkable for its unusual growth rhythm: unlike most succulents, aeoniums grow actively in cool, moist winters and go dormant in summer heat — the rosettes may drop most of their leaves and appear stressed in hot weather but revive with fall rains. The plant produces branching, succulent stems topped with large, flat rosettes of spoon-shaped leaves in bright green ('normal' form), or deep burgundy-purple to nearly black in the popular 'Zwartkop' (Black Rose) cultivar. Mature plants eventually produce a central rosette that elongates into a tall (2–3 foot) cone of small yellow flowers — after which that rosette dies (monocarpic), but side branches continue. The 'Zwartkop' cultivar with nearly black foliage is one of the most dramatically dark-leafed plants in horticulture. In zone 9, aeoniums perform well in the mild climate; avoid summer irrigation. An outstanding architectural container or rock garden plant.
sources: Cactus and Succulent Society of America
University of California Cooperative Extension — Succulents

planting.note: Plant stem or leaf cuttings in spring in very well-drained, lean, gritty, slightly alkaline to neutral succulent mix in full sun to part shade; ghost plant is one of the most cold-tolerant of the echeveria-type succulents, withstanding brief drops to 20°F when dry — and one of the easiest succulents to propagate from individual dropped leaves.

directions: Plant stem cuttings or individual leaves in very well-drained, lean, gritty succulent mix in full sun to part shade; allow to dry between waterings. Graptopetalum paraguayense (ghost plant or mother of pearl plant) is a Mexican succulent closely related to echeverias — producing flat, open rosettes of thick, triangular, pointed leaves in a distinctive pale-blue, grey-lavender, or pinkish color (shifting with light levels and season) with a pearlescent, ghostly quality that gives the plant its evocative common name. The leaves are 2–3 inches long, arranged in a symmetric rosette, and have a chalky, powdery surface texture. Ghost plant is remarkable for its propagation ease — individual leaves dropped from the rosette readily produce new rosettes at the base, making it one of the simplest succulents to multiply. It also tends to trail over time, producing stems up to 12 inches long with rosettes at each node, making it excellent for hanging baskets or draping over rock walls. Ghost plant is significantly more cold-hardy than most succulents in the Crassulaceae family, surviving brief freezes to 20°F when the soil is dry — making it an excellent outdoor succulent for zone 7–11 rock gardens and raised beds.
sources: Cactus and Succulent Society of America
University of California Cooperative Extension — Succulent Plants
North Carolina State Extension — Succulents for Southern Gardens
